title: ES Modules
summary: Split code across files with import and export — named exports, default exports, and how modules keep scope clean.

blocks.md: As programs grow you split them across files. **ES Modules** are the standard way to share code between files with `export` and `import`. Each module has its own scope — nothing leaks into the global namespace unless you export it.

blocks.caption: math.js — named exports
app.js — import what you need

blocks.md: **Named exports** are imported by their exact name inside braces. You can rename on import with `as`: `import { add as sum } from './math.js'`.

blocks.caption: one main export per module

blocks.title: Named vs default
blocks.md: Use **named exports** when a file exposes several things (utilities, constants). Use a **default export** for a module's single main thing (a component, a class). A file can have many named exports and at most one default.

blocks.question: How do you import a default export from './Button.js'?
blocks.explanation: Default exports are imported without braces, and you choose the local name: `import Button from './Button.js'`. Braces are for named exports.

keyTakeaways: Modules have their own scope — share code only via `export`.
Named exports import by name in braces: `import { add } from './math.js'` (rename with `as`).
A default export imports without braces and you name it yourself.
A file can have many named exports but only one default export.
In browsers, use `<script type="module">`; in Node, `.mjs` or `"type": "module"`.

exercise.prompt: In `utils.js`, export a named function `capitalize(s)` and a default function `slugify(s)`. Then write the two import lines in `app.js` to bring both in.
exercise.hint: Named: `export function capitalize...`. Default: `export default function slugify...`. Import default without braces, named with braces.
